claims.text: A foundational survey pick for the corpus is "A Survey of Useful LLM Evaluation," which proposes a two-stage framework progressing from core ability (reasoning, societal impact, domain knowledge) to agent (embodied action, planning, tool learning), anchoring LLM evaluation methodology across the core-to-agent spectrum. It is paired with "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents," which is foundational for the agent evaluation theme and catalogues early agent benchmarks such as ToolAlpaca, APIBench, ToolBench, and the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v1 (BFCL), establishing baseline metrics like pass rates and structural accuracy for one-step tool use.

items.title: Corpus candidate search

items.span_text: Early evaluation efforts offered approaches to evaluate the above sub-tasks while focusing on relatively simple, one-step interactions with explicitly provided parameters. Benchmarks such as ToolAlpaca (Tang et al., 2023), APIBench (Patil et al., 2025b), ToolBench (Qin et al., 2023), and the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v1 (BFCL) (Yan et al., 2024) exemplify this phase, employing synthetic datasets and rule-based matching (e.g., via Abstract Syntax Trees) to establish baseline metrics like pass rates and structural accuracy.
items.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

items.span_text: LLMs have gotten attention across various research domains due to their exceptional performance on a wide range of complex tasks. Therefore, refined methods to evaluate the capabilities of LLMs are needed to determine the tasks and responsibility they should undertake. Our study mainly discussed how LLMs, as useful tools, should be effectively assessed. We proposed the two-stage framework: from �core ability� to �agent�, clearly explaining how LLMs can be applied based on their specific capabilities, along with the evaluation methods in each stage. Core ability refers to the capabilities that LLMs need in order to generate highquality natural language texts. After confirming LLMs possess core ability, they can solve real world and complex tasks as agent. In the "core ability" stage, we discussed the reasoning ability, societal impact, and domain knowledge of LLMs. In the �agent� stage, we demonstrated embodied action, planning, and tool learning of LLMs agent applications. Finally, we examined the challenges currently confronting the evaluation methods for LLMs, as well as the directions for future development.
items.title: A Survey of Useful LLM Evaluation

fields.survey_picks: ["A Survey of Useful LLM Evaluation (a96e1a7c10024c45b186dde5a3ce0122) - anchors the corpus with a core-ability-to-agent evaluation framework covering reasoning, societal impact, domain knowledge, embodied action, planning, and tool learning; foundational for LLM evaluation methodology", "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents (8b9e2f64d565137ca85ae75029e77fff) - foundational for agent evaluation, cataloguing early tool-use benchmarks (ToolAlpaca, APIBench, ToolBench, BFCL v1) and establishing baseline pass-rate and structural-accuracy metrics"]

final_answer.markdown:
1. A foundational survey pick for the corpus is "A Survey of Useful LLM Evaluation," which proposes a two-stage framework progressing from core ability (reasoning, societal impact, domain knowledge) to agent (embodied action, planning, tool learning), anchoring LLM evaluation methodology across the core-to-agent spectrum. It is paired with "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents," which is foundational for the agent evaluation theme and catalogues early agent benchmarks such as ToolAlpaca, APIBench, ToolBench, and the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard v1 (BFCL), establishing baseline metrics like pass rates and structural accuracy for one-step tool use. [1][2]

Sources
[1] A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents, B.2 Function Calling & Tool Use, p. 22
[2] A Survey of Useful LLM Evaluation, Abstract, p. 1
